examples.explanation: i=1: divisors=1 (1), max_div=1, result=1; i=2: divisors=2 (1,2), max_div=2, result=2; i=3: divisors=2 (1,3), max_div=2, result=2; i=4: divisors=3 (1,2,4), max_div=3, result=4; i=5: divisors=2 (1,5), no update; i=6: divisors=4 (1,2,3,6), max_div=4, result=6; i=7: divisors=2 (1,7); i=8: divisors=4 (1,2,4,8), max_div=4, result=6 (tie, keep first); i=9: divisors=3 (1,3,9); i=10: divisors=4 (1,2,5,10), tie. Final result=6.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n * sqrt(n))
approaches.summary: Iterate through numbers up to n, and for each number, count its divisors by checking all integers up to its square root. Use this to track the number with the maximum divisors. Suitable for small n where simplicity is prioritized over efficiency.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Highly Composite Number Using Brute Force
ladders.desc: Given an integer n, find the number ≤ n with the maximum number of divisors using O(n√n) brute force.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is straightforward: to find the number with the most divisors up to n, we need to examine every number from 1 to n, count how many divisors each has, and track the maximum. For each number i, we count its divisors by checking all integers from 1 to √i. If j divides i, we increment the divisor count by 1 (if j = i/j) or 2 (if j ≠ i/j). This works because divisors come in pairs (j, i/j) except when i is a perfect square. The algorithm initializes max_divisors = 0 and result = 1. For i from 1 to n: count divisors of i using the √i method, update max_divisors and result if count > max_divisors. Time complexity is O(n√n) since for each of n numbers, we do O(√i) operations, and O(√i) ≤ O(√n). Space complexity is O(1) as we only store a few variables. This connects to L1 where we focus on the divisor counting subroutine. Common mistakes include not handling perfect squares correctly (counting the square root twice), starting max_divisors at 0 instead of 1 (for n=1), or using inefficient divisor counting. Real code pattern: def highly_composite(n): max_div = 0; result = 1; for i in range(1, n+1): count = 0; for j in range(1, int(i**0.5)+1): if i % j == 0: count += 1 if j != i//j else 2; if count > max_div: max_div = count; result = i; return result.

examples.trace: i=1: divisors=1 (1), max_div=1, result=1; i=2: divisors=2 (1,2), max_div=2, result=2; i=3: divisors=2 (1,3), max_div=2, result=2; i=4: divisors=3 (1,2,4), max_div=3, result=4; i=5: divisors=2 (1,5), no update; i=6: divisors=4 (1,2,3,6), max_div=4, result=6; i=7: divisors=2 (1,7); i=8: divisors=4 (1,2,4,8), max_div=4, result=6 (tie, keep first); i=9: divisors=3 (1,3,9); i=10: divisors=4 (1,2,5,10), tie. Final result=6.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Count Divisors of a Number
ladders.desc: Given an integer x, count its total divisors using the √x optimization.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that divisors of x come in pairs (d, x/d) where d ≤ √x, so we only need to check up to √x. For each integer i from 1 to √x, if i divides x (x % i == 0), then we have found a divisor pair: i and x/i. We increment the count by 2 if i ≠ x/i (distinct pair), or by 1 if i = x/i (perfect square case). This reduces the complexity from O(x) to O(√x). Step-by-step: initialize count = 0; for i from 1 to floor(√x): if x % i == 0: if i == x/i: count += 1 else: count += 2; return count. Time: O(√x), Space: O(1). This feeds into L0 by being called for each i from 1 to n. Common mistakes: using floating-point sqrt without integer conversion, off-by-one in loop range, or mishandling perfect squares. Real code: def count_divisors(x): cnt = 0; limit = int(x**0.5); for i in range(1, limit+1): if x % i == 0: cnt += 1 if i == x//i else 2; return cnt.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Divisor Pair Optimization Using Square Root
ladders.desc: Understand why checking up to √x is sufficient to count all divisors.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that if d is a divisor of x, then x/d is also a divisor, forming a pair (d, x/d). For any pair, one divisor is ≤ √x and the other is ≥ √x. Proof: if d ≤ √x, then x/d ≥ √x because d * (x/d) = x and if both were < √x, product < x, contradiction. Similarly, if d > √x, then x/d < √x. Thus, by iterating d from 1 to √x, we capture all unique divisor pairs. When d = √x exactly (x is perfect square), the pair is (d, d) – same divisor counted once. This reduces the search space dramatically. Algorithm: for d = 1 to √x: if x % d == 0: we have divisors d and x/d. Time: O(√x), Space: O(1). This is the foundation for L1's divisor counting. Common mistakes: not proving the property, or misapplying it to non-integer cases. Real code: same as L1 but focusing on the pair logic.

examples.trace: √25=5, limit=5. d=1: 25%1=0, pair (1,25); d=2: no; d=3: no; d=4: no; d=5: 25%5=0, pair (5,5) – same divisor. Total divisors: 3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterate and Check Divisibility
ladders.desc: Given a range [1, m] and a number x, find all integers in the range that divide x.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves iterating through a sequence and testing a condition (divisibility). For each candidate divisor d in 1 to m, we check if x % d == 0. If true, d is a divisor. This builds the loop structure for divisor counting. Intuition: division is the inverse of multiplication; d divides x if remainder is zero. Step-by-step: initialize list divisors = []; for d from 1 to m: if x % d == 0: append d to divisors; return divisors. Time: O(m), Space: O(k) where k is number of divisors. This feeds into L2 by being the mechanism to find divisor pairs up to √x. Common mistakes: incorrect range (off-by-one), using float division, or not handling edge cases like x=0. Real code: def find_divisors_up_to(x, m): divs = []; for d in range(1, m+1): if x % d == 0: divs.append(d); return divs.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Check Divisibility (Modulo Operation)
ladders.desc: Given two integers a and b, determine if a is divisible by b (i.e., b divides a).
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation tests whether one number divides another without remainder. In programming, we use the modulo operator %: a % b returns the remainder when a is divided by b. If a % b == 0, then b divides a. Intuition: division a/b yields an integer if remainder is zero. Step-by-step: compute remainder = a % b; if remainder == 0, return True (divisible), else False. Time: O(1), Space: O(1). This is used in L3's loop to test each candidate divisor. Common mistakes: confusing % with /, not handling b=0 (division by zero), or using floating-point for integers. Real code: def is_divisible(a, b): return a % b == 0.

examples.trace: 10 % 2 = 0, remainder is zero, so 10 is divisible by 2.
7 % 3 = 1, remainder is non-zero, so 7 is not divisible by 3.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Integer Division and Remainder
ladders.desc: Understand basic arithmetic: dividing integers to get quotient and remainder.
ladders.explanation: For beginners, integer division involves splitting a number into equal parts. Given integers a (dividend) and b (divisor, b≠0), we can express a = b * q + r, where q is the quotient (integer result of a//b) and r is the remainder (0 ≤ r < b). Example: 10 ÷ 3 = 3 with remainder 1, because 10 = 3*3 + 1. The remainder r is what's left after dividing as much as possible. In programming, // gives quotient, % gives remainder. Intuition: if remainder is 0, a is exactly divisible by b. Step-by-step: to compute remainder, subtract b*q from a, where q = floor(a/b). Time: O(1), Space: O(1). This is the prerequisite for L4's modulo operation. Common mistakes: negative numbers (remainder is non-negative in math), or confusion with float division. Real code: q = a // b; r = a % b.

examples.trace: 20 ÷ 5: 5*4=20, remainder = 20-20=0, so 20 is divisible by 5.

approaches.name: Sieve of Eratosthenes O(n log log n)
approaches.summary: Use a modified Sieve of Eratosthenes to precompute the number of divisors for all numbers up to n by iteratively adding divisors. Then, scan the array to find the number with the maximum divisors. Efficient for medium-sized n due to reduced repeated calculations.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Highly Composite Number Using Modified Sieve
ladders.desc: Given n, find the number ≤ n with maximum divisors using modified Sieve of Eratosthenes.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the number with the maximum divisors among all numbers up to n. A naive approach would factor each number individually, which is O(n√n) — too slow for n up to 10^6. The modified Sieve approach precomputes divisor counts for all numbers in O(n log log n) by leveraging the fact that divisors can be built incrementally. The intuition: for each number i, it contributes to the divisor counts of all its multiples. Starting with divisor counts initialized to 0, we iterate i from 1 to n, and for each multiple j of i (j = i, 2i, 3i, ... ≤ n), we increment divisor_count[j] by 1. This works because i is a divisor of j. After building the array, we scan to find the number with maximum divisor_count, handling ties by choosing the smaller number. Time: O(n log log n) due to harmonic series sum (n/1 + n/2 + n/3 + ... ≈ n log n, but optimized by stopping at √n for primality sieve pattern; here we use full multiples so it's O(n log n), but with careful analysis it's O(n log log n) similar to the classic sieve). Space: O(n) for the divisor_count array. Connection to L1: This uses the divisor counting subroutine from L1 as the core building block. Common mistakes: Not initializing counts to 0, incorrect loop bounds causing index errors, not handling ties correctly, and misunderstanding the time complexity. Real code pattern: Initialize array div_cnt of size n+1 with 0s. For i from 1 to n: for j from i to n step i: div_cnt[j] += 1. Then find max index where div_cnt[index] is maximum (with tie-breaking).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Count Divisors for All Numbers Using Incremental Updates
ladders.desc: Given n, compute an array where element i contains the number of divisors of i, for all i from 1 to n.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the heart of the modified Sieve approach. Instead of factoring each number separately, we build the divisor counts collectively. The intuition: every number i is a divisor of its multiples. So, if we iterate i from 1 to n, and for each i, we add 1 to the divisor count of all multiples of i (i, 2i, 3i, ... ≤ n). This ensures that by the end, each number j has received increments from all its divisors. Step-by-step: Initialize an array div_cnt of size n+1 with zeros. For i from 1 to n: For j from i to n, step i: div_cnt[j] += 1. After the loops, div_cnt[j] holds the number of divisors of j. Time: O(n log n) because for each i, the number of multiples is floor(n/i), and sum_{i=1}^n n/i = n * H_n ≈ n log n. With optimization (similar to Sieve of Eratosthenes), it's O(n log log n). Space: O(n). Connection to L0: This array is used in L0 to find the maximum. Connection to L2: This builds on the core logic of marking multiples. Common mistakes: Starting j from 0 instead of i, using wrong step size, or not initializing the array. Real code pattern: div_cnt = [0]*(n+1); for i in range(1, n+1): for j in range(i, n+1, i): div_cnt[j] += 1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterate and Mark Multiples to Accumulate Counts
ladders.desc: Given a number i and limit n, increment counts for all multiples of i up to n.
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique: for a fixed i, we visit all numbers j that are multiples of i (i.e., j = i, 2i, 3i, ... ≤ n) and increment a counter for each j. The intuition: by doing this for all i from 1 to n, each number j ends up with increments from all its divisors, because if d divides j, then j is a multiple of d, so when i=d, j gets incremented. This transforms the problem from per-number factorization to collective updates. Step-by-step: For a given i, set j = i. While j ≤ n: increment count[j], then j += i. Pseudocode: for j in range(i, n+1, i): count[j] += 1. Time: For a single i, it's O(n/i) operations. Over all i, this sums to O(n log n). Space: O(1) extra for this sub-problem (assuming count array exists). Connection to L1: This is the inner loop of L1. Connection to L3: This uses the building block of generating multiples. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in loop bounds, using multiplication instead of addition for incrementing j, or not handling i=0. Real code pattern: for j in range(i, n+1, i): count[j] += 1.

ladders.title: Building Block — Generate Multiples of a Number Within a Limit
ladders.desc: Given a number i and limit n, list all multiples of i that are ≤ n.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is about generating the sequence i, 2i, 3i, ... up to the largest multiple ≤ n. The intuition: multiples are numbers that can be divided by i without remainder, and they form an arithmetic progression with step i. This is fundamental because in the sieve approach, we need to visit these multiples to update divisor counts. Step-by-step: Start with current = i. While current ≤ n: output current, then current += i. Pseudocode: multiples = []; curr = i; while curr ≤ n: multiples.append(curr); curr += i. Time: O(n/i) since number of multiples is floor(n/i). Space: O(n/i) to store the list, or O(1) if just iterating. Connection to L2: This generates the list that L2 iterates over to increment counts. Connection to L4: This uses addition as the basic operation. Common mistakes: Including 0 as a multiple (if starting from 0), or going beyond n due to off-by-one in the loop condition. Real code pattern: for curr in range(i, n+1, i): yield curr or process curr.

examples.trace: Start curr = 3. 3 ≤ 10 → output 3. curr += 3 → 6. 6 ≤ 10 → output 6. curr += 3 → 9. 9 ≤ 10 → output 9. curr += 3 → 12. 12 > 10 → stop. Output list [3,6,9].
Start curr = 5. 5 ≤ 20 → output 5. curr = 10 → output 10. curr = 15 → output 15. curr = 20 → output 20. curr = 25 > 20 → stop. Output [5,10,15,20].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Increment an Array Element by Index
ladders.desc: Given an array and an index, increase the value at that index by 1.
ladders.explanation: This is a fundamental operation used throughout the sieve: we maintain an array where each position corresponds to a number, and we increment the value at that position to count divisors. The intuition: arrays allow O(1) access and update by index, making them efficient for accumulation. Step-by-step: Have an array arr of size m. To increment at index idx (0 ≤ idx < m): arr[idx] = arr[idx] + 1. Pseudocode: arr[idx] += 1. Time: O(1). Space: O(1) extra. Connection to L3: When generating multiples in L3, we use this operation to update the count for each multiple. Connection to L5: This relies on array indexing, a basic programming concept. Common mistakes: Accessing out-of-bounds indices, not initializing the array, or using the wrong index (e.g., off-by-one). Real code pattern: counts = [0]*n; counts[i] += 1.

examples.trace: Start arr = [0,0,0,0]. Index 2 is valid (0-based). arr[2] = arr[2] + 1 = 0 + 1 = 1. So arr becomes [0,0,1,0].
Start arr = [5,3,7,1]. Index 1 is valid. arr[1] = 3 + 1 = 4. So arr becomes [5,4,7,1].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Loops
ladders.desc: Access and modify elements in an array using indices, within a loop structure.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: understanding how to use arrays (or lists) and loops together. An array is a collection of elements accessible by an index (usually starting at 0). A loop allows repeating operations. In the sieve, we use a loop to iterate through indices and update array values. Intuition: Think of an array as a row of boxes numbered 0,1,2,...; you can put numbers in boxes and change them. A loop lets you visit each box one by one. Step-by-step: Initialize an array of size k with zeros. Use a for-loop with index i from 0 to k-1: inside the loop, you can read or write array[i]. Pseudocode: arr = [0]*k; for i in range(k): arr[i] = something. Time: O(k) for the loop. Space: O(k) for the array. Connection to L4: This provides the structure to perform increments across multiple indices. Common mistakes: Confusing 0-based and 1-based indexing, loop off-by-one errors, or not allocating enough array size. Real code pattern: n = 5; counts = [0]*(n+1); for i in range(1, n+1): counts[i] = 0.

examples.input: Create array of size 3, set each element to its index.
examples.trace: Initialize arr = [0,0,0] of size 3. Loop i from 0 to 2: i=0: arr[0] = 0 → arr = [0,0,0]. i=1: arr[1] = 1 → arr = [0,1,0]. i=2: arr[2] = 2 → arr = [0,1,2].

approaches.name: Prime Factorization with Formula O(n * sqrt(n) / log n)
approaches.summary: For each number up to n, compute its prime factorization using trial division or precomputed primes, then apply the divisor count formula (product of (exponent + 1)). Track the maximum. More efficient than brute force for numbers with many small prime factors.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Highly Composite Numbers Using Prime Factorization
ladders.desc: Given n, find all numbers ≤ n with maximum divisor count using prime factorization approach.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding numbers with the highest number of divisors up to a given limit n. The optimal approach uses prime factorization with the divisor count formula. For each number from 1 to n, we compute its prime factorization using trial division with precomputed primes up to √n. Then we apply the divisor count formula: if number = p₁^a₁ × p₂^a₂ × ... × pₖ^aₖ, then divisor count = (a₁+1) × (a₂+1) × ... × (aₖ+1). We track the maximum divisor count and collect all numbers achieving it. The intuition: divisors come from combinations of prime exponents, so factorization reveals all divisor possibilities efficiently. Time complexity is O(n × √n / log n) because we trial divide each number by primes up to √n, and there are about √n / log √n primes. Space is O(√n) for storing primes. Connection to L1: This uses the prime factorization subroutine from L1 repeatedly. Common mistakes: forgetting 1 as a divisor, not handling prime numbers correctly (they have 2 divisors), and inefficient trial division without precomputed primes. Real code pattern: precompute primes with sieve, iterate numbers 1..n, factorize each using trial division with primes, compute divisor count via formula, track max and results.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Precompute primes up to √10 ≈ 3 → [2,3]
Step 2: Process numbers 1-10:
1: factorization {}, divisor count = 1
2: prime 2^1 → (1+1)=2 divisors
3: prime 3^1 → 2 divisors
4: 2^2 → (2+1)=3 divisors
5: prime 5 → 2 divisors
6: 2^1 × 3^1 → (1+1)×(1+1)=4 divisors
7: prime 7 → 2 divisors
8: 2^3 → (3+1)=4 divisors
9: 3^2 → (2+1)=3 divisors
10: 2^1 × 5^1 → 2×2=4 divisors
Step 3: Max divisor count = 4, numbers with 4 divisors: 6,8,10
Step 4: Among these, 6 is smallest, typically we return all: [6,8,10] but problem may ask for smallest or all. We'll return all: [6,8,10]

Step 1: Precompute primes up to √20 ≈ 4 → [2,3]
Step 2: Process numbers, highlight key ones:
1: 1 divisor
2: 2 divisors
3: 2 divisors
4: 3 divisors
5: 2 divisors
6: 4 divisors
7: 2 divisors
8: 4 divisors
9: 3 divisors
10: 4 divisors
11: 2 divisors
12: factorization 2^2 × 3^1 → (2+1)×(1+1)=6 divisors
13: 2 divisors
14: 2×7 → 4 divisors
15: 3×5 → 4 divisors
16: 2^4 → 5 divisors
17: 2 divisors
18: 2×3^2 → (1+1)×(2+1)=6 divisors
19: 2 divisors
20: 2^2×5 → (2+1)×(1+1)=6 divisors
Step 3: Max divisor count = 6, numbers with 6 divisors: 12,18,20
Step 4: Return [12,18,20]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Prime Factorization Using Trial Division
ladders.desc: Factorize a given number into prime exponents using trial division with precomputed primes.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine decomposes a number into its prime factors with their exponents. Given a number x and a list of primes up to √x, we repeatedly divide x by each prime while divisible, counting exponents. After trying all primes, if x > 1, it's a remaining prime factor. The intuition: any composite number has a prime factor ≤ its square root, so checking primes up to √x suffices. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize empty factor dictionary. 2) For each prime p in primes list while p×p ≤ x: count how many times x is divisible by p, add exponent to dictionary, update x = x / p^count. 3) If x > 1, it's a prime factor with exponent 1. Time: O(√x / log √x) for the prime checks. Space: O(number of distinct prime factors). Connection to L0: This factorization is called for each number 1..n in the main problem. Common mistakes: not handling the remaining prime after trial division, infinite loops if not updating x properly, and using all numbers instead of just primes for trial division. Real code pattern: function factorize(x, primes): factors={}, for p in primes: if p*p > x: break, count=0, while x%p==0: x//=p, count+=1, if count>0: factors[p]=count, if x>1: factors[x]=1, return factors.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Divisor Count Formula from Prime Factorization
ladders.desc: Compute number of divisors using formula: product of (exponent + 1) for each prime factor.
ladders.explanation: This is the central mathematical insight: if a number N has prime factorization N = p₁^a₁ × p₂^a₂ × ... × pₖ^aₖ, then the total number of positive divisors of N is (a₁ + 1) × (a₂ + 1) × ... × (aₖ + 1). Intuition: Each divisor can be formed by choosing an exponent for each prime factor from 0 to aᵢ, giving (aᵢ+1) choices per prime. Multiplying choices across independent primes gives total combinations via multiplication principle. Step-by-step: 1) Start with divisor count = 1. 2) For each prime factor with exponent exp in the factorization: multiply divisor count by (exp + 1). 3) Return result. Time: O(k) where k is number of distinct primes. Space: O(1). Connection to L1: This formula takes the output from prime factorization (dictionary of prime→exponent) and computes divisor count. Common mistakes: forgetting to add 1 to exponents, multiplying by exponent instead of (exp+1), and not initializing count to 1. Real code pattern: function count_divisors(factors): result=1, for exp in factors.values(): result *= (exp+1), return result.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize result = 1
Step 2: For prime 2 with exponent 2: result *= (2+1) = 1 × 3 = 3
Step 3: For prime 3 with exponent 1: result *= (1+1) = 3 × 2 = 6
Step 4: Return 6
This corresponds to number 2^2 × 3^1 = 12, which has divisors: 1,2,3,4,6,12 (6 total).

Step 1: Initialize result = 1
Step 2: For prime 5 with exponent 3: result *= (3+1) = 1 × 4 = 4
Step 3: Return 4
This corresponds to number 5^3 = 125, which has divisors: 1,5,25,125 (4 total).

ladders.title: Building Block — Generate Primes Up to √n Using Sieve of Eratosthenes
ladders.desc: Precompute all prime numbers up to a given limit using an efficient sieve algorithm.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept generates a list of primes needed for trial division in factorization. The Sieve of Eratosthenes works by iteratively marking multiples of primes as non-prime. Intuition: Start assuming all numbers ≥2 are prime, then for each number, if it's still prime, mark all its multiples as composite—this efficiently filters primes because any composite number has a prime factor ≤ its square root. Step-by-step: 1) Create boolean array is_prime[0..limit] initialized True. 2) Set is_prime[0]=is_prime[1]=False. 3) For i from 2 to √limit: if is_prime[i] is True: for j from i*i to limit step i: set is_prime[j]=False. 4) Collect all i where is_prime[i] is True. Time: O(limit log log limit). Space: O(limit). Connection to L1: This provides the primes list for trial division factorization. Common mistakes: not optimizing by starting j from i*i, including even numbers unnecessarily, and incorrect limit handling. Real code pattern: def sieve(limit): is_prime=[True]*(limit+1), is_prime[0]=is_prime[1]=False, for i in range(2,int(limit**0.5)+1): if is_prime[i]: for j in range(i*i, limit+1, i): is_prime[j]=False, return [i for i,val in enumerate(is_prime) if val].

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize is_prime[0..10] all True, set is_prime[0]=False, is_prime[1]=False
Step 2: i=2: is_prime[2]=True, mark multiples starting from 4: is_prime[4]=False, is_prime[6]=False, is_prime[8]=False, is_prime[10]=False
Step 3: i=3: is_prime[3]=True, mark multiples starting from 9: is_prime[9]=False
Step 4: i=4: is_prime[4]=False, skip
Step 5: i=5: √10≈3.16, so stop loop (i up to 3)
Step 6: Collect primes: indices 2,3,5,7 where is_prime=True → [2,3,5,7]

Step 1: Initialize is_prime[0..20] all True, set is_prime[0]=False, is_prime[1]=False
Step 2: i=2: mark multiples 4,6,8,10,12,14,16,18,20 as False
Step 3: i=3: mark multiples 9,15 as False (6 already False)
Step 4: i=4: is_prime[4]=False, skip
Step 5: i=5: √20≈4.47, stop loop (i up to 4)
Step 6: Collect primes: indices where True: 2,3,5,7,11,13,17,19 → [2,3,5,7,11,13,17,19]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Trial Division to Check Divisibility
ladders.desc: Determine if a number is divisible by another and perform division while possible.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation checks if a number a is divisible by b (i.e., a % b == 0) and repeatedly divides a by b while divisible. Intuition: Divisibility means b divides a evenly with no remainder; repeated division extracts all factors of b from a. Step-by-step for checking divisibility: compute a % b, if result is 0, then b divides a. For repeated division: while a % b == 0, set a = a // b and count divisions. Time: O(log_b a) for repeated division. Space: O(1). Connection to L1: This is used within prime factorization to count exponents of each prime. Common mistakes: using floating-point division instead of integer, not updating a correctly in loop, and infinite loops if condition doesn't change. Real code pattern: def divide_while_divisible(a, b): count=0, while a % b == 0: a //= b, count+=1, return count, a.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Check 12 % 2 = 0, so divisible. Initialize count=0, a=12
Step 2: First iteration: 12 % 2 == 0 → True, a = 12 // 2 = 6, count = 1
Step 3: Second iteration: 6 % 2 == 0 → True, a = 6 // 2 = 3, count = 2
Step 4: Third iteration: 3 % 2 == 1 ≠ 0 → False, exit loop
Step 5: Return count=2, remaining a=3

Step 1: Check 17 % 3 = 2 ≠ 0, so not divisible initially
Step 2: Since condition a % b == 0 is False from start, loop doesn't execute
Step 3: Return count=0, remaining a=17

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Prime Numbers and Divisibility
ladders.desc: Understand what prime numbers are and basic divisibility rules.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: a prime number is a natural number greater than 1 that has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself. Examples: 2,3,5,7,11. A composite number has more than two divisors. Divisibility: a number a is divisible by b if a ÷ b gives an integer with no remainder, i.e., a % b == 0. Intuition: Primes are building blocks of numbers—every integer >1 is either prime or can be uniquely factored into primes (Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic). Divisibility checks are basic arithmetic operations. Step-by-step to check if n is prime: if n ≤ 1, not prime; for i from 2 to √n, if n % i == 0, then n is composite; otherwise prime. Time: O(√n). Space: O(1). Connection to L4: This foundation underlies trial division, which uses divisibility to find prime factors. Common mistakes: considering 1 as prime, not checking up to √n for efficiency, and misunderstanding divisibility with zero. Real code pattern: def is_prime(n): if n<2: return False, for i in range(2,int(n**0.5)+1): if n%i==0: return False, return True.

examples.trace:
Step 1: n=7 > 1, proceed
Step 2: Check divisibility from i=2 to √7≈2.64 → i=2 only
Step 3: 7 % 2 = 1 ≠ 0, so not divisible by 2
Step 4: No other i to check, so 7 is prime

Step 1: n=9 > 1, proceed
Step 2: Check i=2: 9 % 2 = 1 ≠ 0
Step 3: Check i=3: 9 % 3 = 0 → divisible, so 9 is composite (since 3×3=9)

approaches.name: Recursive Search with Pruning O(2^k)
approaches.summary: Generate highly composite numbers by recursively exploring combinations of prime exponents, using properties like exponents must be non-increasing. Prune branches that exceed n or are less optimal. Best for very large n where generating all numbers is infeasible.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(2^k) where k is the number of primes considered

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Highly Composite Numbers Using Recursive Search with Pruning
ladders.desc: Find all highly composite numbers up to n using recursive exploration of prime exponent combinations with pruning.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding numbers with the maximum number of divisors up to a given limit n. The recursive search with pruning approach efficiently generates candidate numbers by exploring combinations of prime exponents while maintaining the property that exponents must be non-increasing (since rearranging exponents doesn't change the divisor count, but we want the smallest number with that divisor count). We start with the smallest primes and recursively try all possible exponents for each prime, ensuring the current number doesn't exceed n and the exponent sequence is non-increasing. We prune branches where the current number multiplied by the next prime (with the same exponent) would exceed n, or where the divisor count can't exceed the current best. The intuition is that highly composite numbers have prime factorizations where exponents decrease as primes increase, and we only need to explore a fraction of all possible numbers. The algorithm: 1. Generate a list of primes up to enough to cover n. 2. Initialize best_divisors = 0, result = []. 3. Define recursive function dfs(idx, current_val, last_exp, current_divisors, exponents_list). 4. Base case: if current_val > n, return. 5. If current_divisors > best_divisors, update best and reset result with current_val; if equal, append. 6. For next exponent from 1 to last_exp (inclusive), compute new_val = current_val * (prime[idx]^exp). 7. If new_val <= n, recursively call dfs with idx+1, new_val, exp, current_divisors*(exp+1), exponents_list+[exp]. 8. Start with dfs(0, 1, infinity, 1, []). Time: O(2^k) where k is number of primes considered (worst-case exploring all exponent combinations), but pruning reduces it significantly. Space: O(k) for recursion depth and storing exponents. Connection: This integrates all lower-level concepts into a complete solution. Common mistakes: Forgetting to enforce non-increasing exponents (leading to duplicate numbers with same divisor count), not pruning effectively (causing timeout for large n), or mishandling when divisor counts tie. Real code pattern: def find_highly_composite(n): primes = [2,3,5,7,11,13,...]; best = 0; res = []; def dfs(i, val, last_exp, divs): nonlocal best, res; if val > n: return; if divs > best: best = divs; res = [val]; elif divs == best: res.append(val); for e in range(1, last_exp+1): new_val = val * (primes[i]**e); if new_val <= n: dfs(i+1, new_val, e, divs*(e+1)); dfs(0, 1, float('inf'), 1); return sorted(res).

examples.trace: Primes considered: [2,3,5]. Start: dfs(0,1,inf,1). val=1 <=10, divs=1, best=0 -> update best=1, res=[1]. Try exponents for prime 2: e=1: new_val=2, dfs(1,2,1,2). val=2 <=10, divs=2 > best=1 -> update best=2, res=[2]. For prime 3: e=1 (<=last_exp=1): new_val=6, dfs(2,6,1,4). val=6 <=10, divs=4 > best=2 -> update best=4, res=[6]. For prime 5: e=1: new_val=30 >10, prune. e=2: skip as e>last_exp. Backtrack: e=2 for prime 2: new_val=4, dfs(1,4,2,3). val=4 <=10, divs=3 > best=4? no (3<4). For prime 3: e=1 (<=2): new_val=12 >10, prune; e=2: new_val=36 >10. Backtrack: e=3 for prime 2: new_val=8, dfs(1,8,3,4). val=8 <=10, divs=4 == best=4 -> res=[6,8]. For prime 3: e=1: new_val=24 >10. Continue other branches similarly. Final res after sorting: [6] (since 6 has 4 divisors, 8 also has 4 but 6<8, but we collect all with max divisors; actually 6 and 8 both have 4 divisors, so output should be [6,8]. Let's correct: During trace, at val=8, divs=4 == best=4, so res becomes [6,8]. Other numbers: 9 has 3 divisors, 10 has 4 divisors? 10=2*5, divisors: 1,2,5,10 -> 4 divisors. So 10 should be included. Let's trace: From start, branch with prime 2 e=1, prime 3 e=1 gave 6. Branch with prime 2 e=1, prime 5 e=1: new_val=10, dfs(2,10,1,4) from val=2: val=10 <=10, divs=4 == best=4 -> res=[6,8,10]. So output is [6,8,10]. But 6 has divisors 1,2,3,6 (4); 8 has 1,2,4,8 (4); 10 has 1,2,5,10 (4). So all have 4 divisors, max is 4. So final answer: [6,8,10]. Actually, the problem typically returns numbers with max divisors, so for n=10, numbers with 4 divisors are 6,8,10. So output: [6,8,10].

Primes: [2,3,5,7,11,13,...]. Start dfs(0,1,inf,1): val=1, divs=1, best=0 -> update best=1, res=[1]. Prime 2 exponents: e=1: val=2, dfs(1,2,1,2): divs=2 >1 -> best=2, res=[2]. Prime 3: e=1: val=6, dfs(2,6,1,4): divs=4 >2 -> best=4, res=[6]. Prime 5: e=1: val=30>20, prune. Backtrack: e=2 for prime 2: val=4, dfs(1,4,2,3): divs=3 <4, so no update. Prime 3: e=1 (<=2): val=12, dfs(2,12,1,6): divs=6 >4 -> best=6, res=[12]. Prime 5: e=1: val=60>20. e=2: val=100>20. Continue: e=3 for prime 2: val=8, dfs(1,8,3,4): divs=4 <6. Prime 3: e=1: val=24>20. e=4: val=16, dfs(1,16,4,5): divs=5 <6. Explore other branches: From val=2, prime 3 e=1 gave 6; prime 5 e=1: val=10, dfs(2,10,1,4): divs=4 <6. From val=4, prime 3 e=2: val=36>20. From val=6, prime 5 e=1: val=30>20. Also val=12 from earlier. Check val=18: 18=2*3^2, divisors: (1+1)*(2+1)=6, same as 12, but 12<18, so 12 is in res. val=20=2^2*5, divisors: (2+1)*(1+1)=6, so 20 also has 6 divisors. So res should include 12 and 20. Let's trace: At val=12, best=6, res=[12]. Later, from val=4, prime 5 e=1: val=20, dfs(2,20,1,6): divs=6 == best=6 -> res=[12,20]. So output: [12,20]. But 12 has divisors 1,2,3,4,6,12 (6); 20 has 1,2,4,5,10,20 (6). So both have 6 divisors. So final answer: [12,20].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive DFS with Exponent Tracking
ladders.desc: Implement the depth-first search that explores prime exponent combinations with non-increasing constraint.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core recursive function that generates numbers by multiplying primes with decreasing exponents. The intuition is that to efficiently generate numbers with many divisors, we need to try all valid combinations of exponents for a sequence of primes, where each exponent is at most the previous one (non-increasing). This ensures we generate each unique divisor count with the smallest possible number. The algorithm: function dfs(idx, current_val, last_exp, current_divisors): if current_val > n: return; if current_divisors > best_divisors: update best and result; else if equal: append to result; for exp from 1 to last_exp: new_val = current_val * (primes[idx]^exp); if new_val <= n: dfs(idx+1, new_val, exp, current_divisors*(exp+1)); Start with dfs(0, 1, infinity, 1). Time: O(2^k) in worst case as each prime can have multiple exponent choices, but pruning reduces it. Space: O(k) for recursion stack. Connection: This function is called within the full solution to traverse the search space. Common mistakes: Using increasing exponents (wastes time on duplicates), not limiting exponents based on last_exp, or incorrect divisor count update. Real code pattern: def dfs(i, val, last_exp, divs): if val > n: return; if divs > best: best = divs; res = [val]; elif divs == best: res.append(val); for e in range(1, last_exp+1): new_val = val * (primes[i]**e); if new_val <= n: dfs(i+1, new_val, e, divs*(e+1)).

examples.input: primes=[2,3], n=10, start: idx=0, val=1, last_exp=inf, divs=1
examples.output: Explores numbers: 2,4,8,6,12,18,36, etc., but within n=10
examples.trace: Call dfs(0,1,inf,1). val=1 <=10. Try prime 2: e=1: new_val=2, call dfs(1,2,1,2). val=2 <=10. For prime 3: e=1 (<=1): new_val=6, call dfs(2,6,1,4). idx=2 is beyond primes list, so base case? Actually, we need to handle when idx == len(primes): return. So at dfs(2,6,1,4): idx=2 == len(primes)=2, so return. Backtrack: e=2 for prime 2: new_val=4, dfs(1,4,2,3). val=4 <=10. For prime 3: e=1 (<=2): new_val=12 >10, prune; e=2: new_val=36 >10. Backtrack: e=3: new_val=8, dfs(1,8,3,4). val=8 <=10. For prime 3: e=1: new_val=24 >10; e=2: 72>10; e=3: 216>10. So numbers generated: 2,4,8,6. Also from val=1, e=2 for prime 2 gave 4, e=3 gave 8. So DFS explores these paths.

examples.input: primes=[2,3,5], n=20, start: idx=0, val=2, last_exp=1, divs=2
examples.trace: Call dfs(1,2,1,2). val=2 <=20. For prime 3: e=1: new_val=6, dfs(2,6,1,4). val=6 <=20. For prime 5: e=1: new_val=30 >20, prune; so return. Backtrack: e=2 for prime 3? last_exp=1, so e only up to 1. So from val=2, next prime 5: e=1: new_val=10, dfs(2,10,1,4). val=10 <=20. idx=2, prime 5 is current, so for next prime? idx=3 beyond list, return. So from val=2, we get 6 and 10. Also from other branches like val=4, etc.

testCases.input: primes=[2], n=5, start: idx=0, val=1, last_exp=inf, divs=1
primes=[2,3], n=10, start: idx=0, val=1, last_exp=inf, divs=1
primes=[2,3], n=20, start: idx=0, val=1, last_exp=inf, divs=1
primes=[2,3,5], n=30, start: idx=0, val=1, last_exp=inf, divs=1
primes=[2,3,5], n=50, start: idx=0, val=1, last_exp=inf, divs=1
primes=[2,3,5,7], n=100, start: idx=0, val=1, last_exp=inf, divs=1
primes=[2], n=1, start: idx=0, val=1, last_exp=inf, divs=1
testCases.expected: No numbers generated as val=1, e=1 gives 2>1

testCases.input: primes=[2,3], n=3, start: idx=0, val=1, last_exp=inf, divs=1
primes=[2,3,5], n=15, start: idx=0, val=1, last_exp=inf, divs=1
primes=[2,3,5], n=25, start: idx=0, val=1, last_exp=inf, divs=1

ladders.title: Core Logic — Non-increasing Exponent Property for Divisor Maximization
ladders.desc: Understand why exponents must be non-increasing to find numbers with maximum divisors.
ladders.explanation: The central insight is that for a given set of prime factors, the number of divisors is maximized when the exponents are arranged in non-increasing order. Specifically, if we have primes p1 < p2 < ... < pk, then for the number n = p1^e1 * p2^e2 * ... * pk^ek, the divisor count d(n) = (e1+1)*(e2+1)*...*(ek+1). To maximize d(n) for a fixed n or to find the smallest n with a given d(n), we should have e1 >= e2 >= ... >= ek. The intuition: Larger exponents on smaller primes yield a smaller number for the same divisor count because smaller primes contribute less to the product. For example, compare 12 = 2^2 * 3^1 (exponents 2,1) and 18 = 2^1 * 3^2 (exponents 1,2). Both have divisor count (2+1)*(1+1)=6 and (1+1)*(2+1)=6, but 12 < 18. So by enforcing non-increasing exponents, we ensure we only consider the smallest number for each divisor count configuration. This property allows pruning: if we try an exponent larger than the previous, we might get a larger number with the same divisor count, which is suboptimal. In the recursive search, we pass the last exponent to limit the next exponent, ensuring the sequence doesn't increase. This reduces the search space significantly. Connection: This logic is used in the DFS to guide exponent choices. Common mistakes: Thinking exponents can be any order, or not realizing that non-increasing order gives the minimal number. Real code pattern: In DFS, last_exp parameter ensures for e in range(1, last_exp+1).

examples.output: (2,1) gives 12, (1,2) gives 18; both have 6 divisors, but 12 is smaller.
examples.trace: For primes 2 and 3. Set exponents (2,1): number = 2^2 * 3^1 = 4*3=12. Divisor count = (2+1)*(1+1)=3*2=6. Set exponents (1,2): number = 2^1 * 3^2 = 2*9=18. Divisor count = (1+1)*(2+1)=2*3=6. Both have 6 divisors, but 12 < 18. So non-increasing order (2,1) yields the smaller number. In recursive search, when we have last_exp=2 for prime 2, we allow exponents for prime 3 up to 2, but if we try (1,2), it would require starting with exponent 1 for prime 2 and then 2 for prime 3, which violates non-increasing if last_exp=1. So we avoid it.

examples.output: (3,2,1) gives 360, (2,3,1) gives 540; both have 24 divisors, but 360 is smaller.
examples.trace: Exponents (3,2,1): number = 2^3 * 3^2 * 5^1 = 8*9*5=360. Divisor count = (3+1)*(2+1)*(1+1)=4*3*2=24. Exponents (2,3,1): number = 2^2 * 3^3 * 5^1 = 4*27*5=540. Divisor count = (2+1)*(3+1)*(1+1)=3*4*2=24. 360 < 540, so non-increasing (3,2,1) is better. In search, we enforce that after exponent 3 for prime 2, exponent for prime 3 can be at most 3, but we choose 2 to keep non-increasing.

ladders.title: Building Block — Compute Divisor Count from Prime Factorization
ladders.desc: Calculate the number of divisors given a list of prime exponents.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves computing the divisor count from the prime factorization of a number. If a number n is expressed as n = p1^e1 * p2^e2 * ... * pk^ek, where p_i are distinct primes and e_i are their exponents, then the total number of divisors d(n) is given by d(n) = (e1+1) * (e2+1) * ... * (ek+1). The intuition: Each divisor can be formed by choosing for each prime p_i an exponent from 0 to e_i inclusive. For prime p_i, there are (e_i+1) choices (since exponent can be 0,1,...,e_i). By the multiplication principle, the total combinations are the product of these choices. This formula is efficient as it requires only the exponents, not the actual divisors. In the recursive search, we update the divisor count incrementally: when we add a new prime with exponent e, we multiply the current divisor count by (e+1). This avoids recomputing from scratch. Time: O(k) to compute from exponents, but O(1) per update in recursion. Space: O(1). Connection: This is used in the DFS to track and compare divisor counts. Common mistakes: Forgetting to add 1 to exponents, or mis-multiplying when exponents are zero. Real code pattern: Given exponents list [e1,e2,...,ek], divs = 1; for e in exponents: divs *= (e+1).

examples.trace: Number is 2^2 * 3^1 = 12. For prime 2: exponents possible: 0,1,2 -> 3 choices. For prime 3: exponents possible: 0,1 -> 2 choices. Total divisors: 3 * 2 = 6. The divisors are: 1 (2^0*3^0), 2 (2^1*3^0), 3 (2^0*3^1), 4 (2^2*3^0), 6 (2^1*3^1), 12 (2^2*3^1).
Number is 2^1 * 3^1 * 5^1 = 30. For prime 2: choices: 0,1 -> 2. For prime 3: choices: 0,1 -> 2. For prime 5: choices: 0,1 -> 2. Total: 2*2*2=8. Divisors: 1,2,3,5,6,10,15,30.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Generate Primes up to a Limit
ladders.desc: Produce a list of prime numbers up to a given bound for use in factorization.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves generating all prime numbers up to a certain limit. In the context of highly composite numbers, we need primes to form the numbers. Since highly composite numbers have many divisors, they are composed of small primes. We typically generate primes up to a sufficient number such that the product of the first few primes exceeds n. For example, for n up to 10^9, we might need primes up to around 50 or so. A common method is the Sieve of Eratosthenes: 1. Create a boolean list is_prime of size limit+1, initialize all to True. 2. Set is_prime[0] and is_prime[1] to False. 3. For i from 2 to sqrt(limit): if is_prime[i] is True, then for j from i*i to limit step i: set is_prime[j] = False. 4. Collect all i where is_prime[i] is True. Time: O(limit log log limit). Space: O(limit). Connection: This list of primes is used in the recursive search to multiply and form numbers. Common mistakes: Not generating enough primes (causing missed numbers), or generating too many (inefficient). Real code pattern: def sieve(limit): is_prime = [True]*(limit+1); is_prime[0]=is_prime[1]=False; for i in range(2, int(limit**0.5)+1): if is_prime[i]: for j in range(i*i, limit+1, i): is_prime[j]=False; return [i for i in range(limit+1) if is_prime[i]].

examples.trace: Initialize is_prime[0..10] all True. Set is_prime[0]=False, is_prime[1]=False. i=2: is_prime[2]=True, mark multiples: 4,6,8,10 as False. i=3: is_prime[3]=True, mark multiples: 6,9 as False (6 already False). i=4: is_prime[4]=False, skip. i=5: sqrt(10)≈3, so stop. Collect primes: 2,3,5,7.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Prime Numbers and Factorization
ladders.desc: Understand what prime numbers are and how to factorize a number into primes.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: prime numbers and prime factorization. A prime number is a natural number greater than 1 that has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself. Examples: 2,3,5,7,11. Every integer greater than 1 can be uniquely expressed as a product of prime numbers, called its prime factorization. For example, 12 = 2 * 2 * 3 = 2^2 * 3^1. To factorize a number n, we repeatedly divide by primes starting from 2. The intuition: primes are the building blocks of numbers, and factorization breaks a number down into these basic components. This is foundational because highly composite numbers are defined by their prime factorization, and the number of divisors depends on the exponents in this factorization. Step-by-step factorization: 1. Start with n and an empty list of factors. 2. For each prime p from 2 upward: while n is divisible by p: add p to factors, divide n by p. 3. Stop when n becomes 1. Time: O(sqrt(n)) for naive division. Space: O(log n) for storing factors. Connection: This concept is used to understand how numbers are constructed from primes, which leads to computing divisor counts. Common mistakes: Thinking 1 is prime, or not handling repeated factors correctly. Real code pattern: def factorize(n): factors = []; p = 2; while p*p <= n: while n % p == 0: factors.append(p); n //= p; p += 1; if n > 1: factors.append(n); return factors.

examples.trace: Start with n=12. p=2: 12 % 2 == 0, so add 2, n=12/2=6. Again, 6 % 2 == 0, add 2, n=6/2=3. p=2: 3 % 2 != 0, so increment p to 3. p=3: 3 % 3 == 0, add 3, n=3/3=1. Stop. Factors: [2,2,3].

n=30. p=2: 30%2==0, add 2, n=15. p=2: 15%2!=0, increment p to 3. p=3: 15%3==0, add 3, n=5. p=3: 5%3!=0, increment p to 4 (not prime, but algorithm checks divisibility by 4, which fails). p=4: 5%4!=0, increment to 5. p=5: 5%5==0, add 5, n=1. Stop. Factors: [2,3,5].
